response:
The area of the regular hexagonal community center is derived by dividing the shape into six equilateral triangles, each with a side length of 30 meters, and applying the trigonometric identity for the area of a triangle involving the sine of 60 degrees. By calculating the area of one triangle as one-half times the square of the side length multiplied by the sine of the internal angle, we find the area of a single triangle to be 225 square root of 3, which when multiplied by six yields a total hexagonal area of 1350 square root of 3 square meters, approximately equal to 2338.27 square meters.

The radius of the circular garden corresponds to the apothem of the hexagon, which is the distance from the center to the midpoint of a side, determined by using the cosine of 30 degrees on the right triangle formed by the radius, half the side length, and the distance from the center to a vertex. This calculation reveals the radius to be 15 square root of 3 meters, and substituting this value into the standard circle area formula of pi times the radius squared results in a garden area of 675 pi square meters, approximately equal to 2120.58 square meters.
